No single AI method supplies all of these conditions. The study needs an
explicit question and scope, inspectable artifacts and tool outputs, an
architectural claim tied to evidence, and a person responsible for the
decision. Making those elements visible allows the architect to choose where AI
belongs and to judge whether it helped.

::: {#pri-treat-feedback .callout-design-principle title="Use AI where it reduces engineering work"}
First identify which part of the work is slowing the study, whether that is
representing the problem, producing valid candidates, obtaining useful
feedback, rejecting weak results, or making a decision. Use AI only when it
reduces that burden without increasing verification, review, or decision
pressure elsewhere.
:::

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Open Research Questions

Each question below grows out of a difficulty examined in this chapter. Some
ask what an AI system must know or be allowed to change. Others address
expensive feedback, review, and the final decision. In every case, the
comparison should be with existing architecture practice, and success should
be judged by the effect on the study rather than the model's output alone.

1. **How can AI tell when missing or stale information makes an architecture
comparison unreliable?**
The software and physical-design examples in this chapter show how a result can
change when its workload, compiler, memory-movement estimate, or physical
assumption changes. Research could use versioned cases in which relevant inputs
are withheld or updated, then test whether an AI system identifies what is
missing and requests the right measurement or tool check. Comparisons with
explicit checklists, change thresholds, and expert review should report missed
rank reversals, unnecessary re-evaluations, and the work required to correct a
flawed comparison.

2. **Can AI distinguish a difficult search from a design space that cannot
satisfy the task?**
Specialization and chiplets create many choices, but a large design space can
still omit the interface, partition, or software path needed to meet the
requirements. Research should test whether an AI system can recognize when more
search is unlikely to help and identify the fixed assumption that blocks
progress. Comparisons with constraint solvers, established search methods, and
expert review could use design spaces whose feasibility is known. The
evaluation should report how often the system avoids invalid evaluations and
whether it correctly identifies which design choice the architect would need
to reconsider.

3. **Can AI spend a fixed high-fidelity evaluation budget better than current
methods?**
The physical constraints examined in this chapter can reverse rankings produced
by inexpensive models, while detailed simulation and implementation remain
costly. A fair comparison would give AI, established search methods, and expert
triage the same design space, tool access, and evaluation budget. It should
measure the quality of the resulting architecture comparison, promising designs
rejected too early, weak designs sent to expensive tools, and total tool cost.
The result would show whether AI makes scarce evaluation more useful rather
than merely changing which candidates consume it.

4. **Can AI help reviewers examine more evidence without hiding
disagreement?**
Faster generation can produce more results than reviewers can inspect, and the
AlphaChip case shows why differences in workloads, baselines, budgets, and tool
settings must remain visible. AI can organize related runs and point reviewers
toward conflicting results, but automatic summaries can also erase those
differences. Reviewers could examine the same real architecture studies with
or without an AI interface linked to the underlying artifacts. The evaluation
should measure review time, unsupported claims detected, important
disagreements missed, and conclusions accepted without sufficient evidence.

5. **Can AI help turn architecture evidence into a decision the evidence
supports?**
A study can remain stuck after evaluation because the evidence has not been
connected to a clear action. AI could organize evidence for and against a
candidate, state unresolved risks, and identify the conditions that would
reopen the decision. Research should compare that assistance with structured
templates and expert preparation on studies with a defined decision scope. The
resulting recommendation should be supported by the evidence, include relevant
counterevidence, and leave the final choice with the responsible architect.

6. **Does AI reduce end-to-end engineering work, or merely move it?**
The compounded challenge described in this chapter means that a local speedup
can create more work in evaluation, tool integration, debugging, verification,
or review. Assisted and unassisted teams could address matched architecture
questions with the same tools, budgets, and decision criteria. The comparison
should include the quality of the resulting study, engineering time, tool cost,
invalid runs, review effort, and time to a decision. Counting the work required
to check the AI system would show whether it reduces the total burden or moves
that burden to a less visible part of the study.

## Conclusion

Architecture has always relied on feedback, and it has repeatedly adapted by
making interfaces, design rules, tool outputs, and comparison standards
explicit. AI now enters a process that must also coordinate specialization,
heterogeneous integration, data movement, changing software, physical
constraints, and expensive validation. These pressures can keep a study from
reaching a defensible decision in several different ways.

The chapter leaves five practical challenges for AI-assisted architecture.

- **Relevant information must be available and current.** AI cannot account for
  a workload, software assumption, interface, or physical constraint that the
  study never represents, and stale assumptions can invalidate an otherwise
  coherent result.
- **The design space must contain valid choices.** Specialization and chiplets
  create many possible designs, but their validity depends on hardware,
  software, packaging, and physical constraints that must be considered
  together.
- **Decisive feedback may remain expensive.** Cheap models and surrogates can
  screen candidates, but omitted physical effects can reverse their rankings,
  while simulation, implementation, and verification consume scarce resources.
- **Review capacity is finite.** Faster generation helps only when weak
  candidates can be rejected and conflicting evidence can be examined before
  they consume more tool time and expert attention.
- **The evidence must support a defined decision.** A result is useful only when
  the responsible architect can say what action it supports and who is
  responsible for taking that action.

AI assistance is useful only when it helps architects manage one or more of
these challenges without weakening the comparison or moving the burden
elsewhere.
Another architect should be able to inspect the assumptions, tool results,
failed and rejected alternatives, evidence, and final decision, then judge
whether the assistance helped. Producing more candidates does not improve a
study when the comparison remains incomplete, the decisive checks remain
unaffordable, or the next action remains unclear.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Match AI's role to a specific architecture challenge and
  measure whether it reduced that burden.
- **Reader test:** Can you explain which challenge or combination of challenges
  limits a study and what evidence would show that AI reduced the burden rather
  than shifted it elsewhere?
- **Up next:** @sec-architecture-20-ontology distinguishes a study's scope,
  artifacts, architectural claim, execution loop, evidence, and decision
  boundary.
:::
